questions.prompt: const fn = obj.method; fn(); — what is this inside fn (strict mode)?
questions.explanation: Implicit binding is lost when a method is extracted. A plain function call uses default binding, which is undefined in strict mode.

questions.prompt: Which method binds this without immediately calling the function?
questions.explanation: bind() returns a new function with this permanently set. call() and apply() invoke immediately.

questions.prompt: Arrow functions: can you change their this with .call()?
options: No — arrow function this is baked in at creation and cannot be overridden
Only if the arrow function is at module scope
questions.explanation: Arrow functions have no own this. Passing a different thisArg to .call(), .apply(), or .bind() has no effect.

questions.prompt: const obj = { name: 'Alice', greet: () => console.log(this.name) }; What does obj.greet() print?
questions.explanation: The arrow function captures this from its definition scope (module scope), where this is undefined (or globalThis.name in global scope, likely undefined).

questions.prompt: What is the highest-priority this-binding rule?
questions.explanation: Priority order: new > explicit (.call/.bind/.apply) > implicit (obj.method()) > default.

questions.prompt: Give TWO ways to pass an object method as a callback while preserving its this.
call() vs apply() — what is the difference?
options: call takes args as array; apply takes args individually
apply takes args as array; call takes args individually
call returns a new function; apply invokes immediately
There is no difference in modern JavaScript
questions.explanation: fn.call(thisArg, arg1, arg2) — individual args. fn.apply(thisArg, [arg1, arg2]) — array of args.
